quizBank.prompt: What is the primary purpose of an operational database?
quizBank.explanation: Operational databases optimize for concurrent transactions, integrity, and low-latency reads/writes.

quizBank.prompt: Which data type best describes a JSON product catalog export?
quizBank.explanation: JSON has structure but flexible schema — classic semi-structured data.

options: Core entities such as customers and products
quizBank.explanation: Master data defines shared core entities used across systems.

quizBank.prompt: Why do organizations use a data warehouse separate from operational databases?
options: Analytical queries can slow transactional systems
Warehouses replace the need for AI
quizBank.explanation: Mixing heavy analytics with OLTP risks performance and availability.
Metadata describes structure, lineage, quality, and governance of data assets.

quizBank.prompt: A ride-sharing app records GPS pings every three seconds. Which category dominates?
quizBank.explanation: High-velocity events are operational/transactional data feeding real-time systems.

quizBank.prompt: Netflix wants quarterly retention analysis across five years. Where should analysts primarily query?
quizBank.explanation: Long-range aggregates belong in analytical stores, not live transaction systems.

quizBank.prompt: A hospital shares patient summaries with insurers using standard codes. This illustrates:
quizBank.explanation: Standard codes and shared patient identity rely on governed master/reference data.

quizBank.prompt: A startup has 50 daily orders and one founder doing books in Excel. The CEO asks for real-time fraud scoring. What is the first gap?
options: They need a graph database immediately
Excel cannot support integrated operational + analytical scale
They should delete all historical data
quizBank.explanation: Excel breaks down for integrated, concurrent, auditable operational workloads.

quizBank.prompt: Spotify playlists update for millions of users each hour. Which statement is most accurate?
options: All playlist data lives inside the language model
Operational, analytical, and recommendation systems cooperate
One MySQL table is always sufficient
quizBank.explanation: Spotify combines transactional history, analytics, and ML — not a single tool.

quizBank.prompt: Which component should handle credit card authorization during checkout?
quizBank.explanation: Authorizations require ACID transactions and low latency — operational tier.

quizBank.prompt: Where should embedding vectors for semantic product search be stored?
quizBank.explanation: Vector workloads need specialized indexes — not spreadsheets.

quizBank.prompt: A nightly job copies orders from MySQL to Snowflake. This step is called:
quizBank.explanation: Integrating operational data into analytical stores is ETL/ELT.

quizBank.prompt: ChatGPT states: 'Companies no longer need SQL databases because I can answer business questions.' Best critique?
options: LLMs lack authoritative, concurrent, auditable records of organizational truth
quizBank.explanation: Models generate text; enterprises require governed systems of record.

quizBank.prompt: An AI lists Amazon's top-selling SKU yesterday without database access. Primary risk?
options: Answer may hallucinate or use stale training data
AI always has live warehouse access by default
quizBank.explanation: Without retrieval over current data, answers may be plausible but wrong.

quizBank.prompt: What assumption about databases changed most for you in this chapter?
Would you recommend eliminating databases for a 500-person e-commerce company? Why or why not?

videos.videoTitle: AWS — What is a Data Warehouse?

references.title: Date, C.J. — An Introduction to Database Systems
Kimball & Ross — The Data Warehouse Toolkit

instructorNotes: Timing: 45 min Opening Story + Think First discussion; 60 min Core Concepts and ecosystem diagram; 45 min Amazon worked example; 30 min hands-on classification lab; 30 min AI critique breakout; 30 min architecture challenge. Emphasize driving question throughout. Do not resolve CEO question until after Section 6.

figures.title: Modern Enterprise Data Ecosystem
figures.purpose: Establish the textbook's foundational mental model connecting business, operational systems, databases, analytics, AI, and decisions.
figures.layout: Vertical stack, six layers, left labels, center flow arrows, Business Universe icons on right margin
figures.caption: Figure 1.1: From business events to decisions — the enterprise data ecosystem introduced in Chapter 1.
figures.alt: Diagram showing Business at top, then Operational Systems, Databases, Analytics, AI, and Business Decisions at bottom, connected by downward arrows.
figures.animationNotes: Reveal layers top-to-bottom; pulse AI layer when introduced; highlight feedback arrow from Decisions to Business.

figures.title: Operational vs Analytical Data
figures.purpose: Distinguish systems optimized for transactions from systems optimized for analysis.
figures.layout: Two-column comparison with center divider; left OLTP icons, right OLAP icons
figures.caption: Figure 1.2: Operational data supports live transactions; analytical data supports questions across time.
figures.alt: Left column shows order placement and payment with millisecond latency; right column shows dashboards and trend charts with batch and interactive queries.
figures.animationNotes: Animate sample order on left; aggregate bars building on right.

figures.title: Amazon Customer Order Journey
figures.purpose: Trace one customer order through website, operational database, warehouse, analytics, AI, and executive dashboard.
figures.layout: Horizontal swimlane: Customer, Website, Operational DB, ETL, Warehouse, ML, Dashboard
figures.caption: Figure 1.3: A single Amazon order generates operational records, analytical history, and recommendation signals.
figures.alt: Flow from customer click through MySQL order tables, nightly ETL to Snowflake, recommendation model, and executive KPI dashboard.
figures.animationNotes: Dot travels along path; labels appear at each system.

figures.title: Organizational Data Lifecycle
figures.purpose: Show create, store, integrate, analyze, govern, and retire stages of enterprise data.
figures.layout: Circular lifecycle with six stages and governance ring overlay
figures.caption: Figure 1.4: Enterprise data lifecycle — from creation to archival with continuous governance.
figures.alt: Circular diagram with Create, Store, Integrate, Analyze, Decide, and Archive stages.
figures.animationNotes: Rotate highlight through stages; governance ring pulses on Store and Analyze.

figures.title: AI Models vs Enterprise Databases
figures.purpose: Clarify that language models reason over patterns while databases store authoritative organizational records.
figures.layout: Side-by-side panels: ChatGPT (ephemeral, probabilistic) vs Database (persistent, transactional)
figures.caption: Figure 1.5: AI models generate answers; databases store the records organizations must trust.
figures.alt: Left panel shows chat interface with probabilistic output; right panel shows tables with ACID properties and audit trail.
figures.animationNotes: Contrast checkmarks for persistence, concurrency, and audit on database side only.

figures.title: Modern Data Architecture Overview
figures.purpose: Preview how multiple database technologies coexist in one architecture.
figures.layout: Hub architecture: sources → operational stores → integration → warehouse/lake → consumption (BI, AI)
figures.caption: Figure 1.6: No single database serves every workload — modern architectures combine specialized systems.
figures.alt: Architecture diagram with MySQL, MongoDB, Kafka, Snowflake, and vector search serving different layers.
figures.animationNotes: Highlight one technology at a time with workload label.

figures.title: Technology Evolution Timeline
figures.purpose: Position Excel, relational databases, cloud databases, warehouses, vector databases, and AI on a timeline.
figures.caption: Figure 1.7: Enterprise data technologies evolved to match growing scale, variety, and intelligence requirements.
figures.alt: Timeline from spreadsheets through relational databases, cloud, warehouses, and vector databases to enterprise AI.
figures.animationNotes: Scroll timeline left-to-right; tooltip on each milestone.

figures.title: Chapter 1 Concept Map
figures.purpose: Summarize relationships among core concepts introduced in this chapter.
figures.layout: Radial concept map with Enterprise Data at center
figures.caption: Figure 1.8: Concept map — how foundational data ideas connect in Chapter 1.
figures.alt: Concept map linking enterprise data, operational data, analytical data, lifecycle, ecosystem, and AI complementarity.

figures.title: Chapter 1 Knowledge Graph
figures.purpose: Show CONCEPT and TECH nodes introduced in this chapter for adaptive learning paths.
figures.layout: Graph view: CH-001 connected to CONCEPT nodes and CASE nodes
figures.caption: Figure 1.9: Knowledge graph entry point for Module 1.
figures.alt: Graph with CH-001 node linked to Amazon, Netflix, Spotify cases and six concept nodes.
figures.animationNotes: Fade in nodes by category: concepts, cases, technologies.

figures.title: Startup Technology Comparison Matrix
figures.purpose: Support Technology Decision section comparing Excel through Snowflake.
figures.layout: Matrix: rows = criteria (scale, cost, skills, analytics, AI); columns = technologies
figures.caption: Figure 1.10: Comparison matrix — selecting data technology by business requirement.
figures.alt: Table comparing Excel, Access, MySQL, MongoDB, and Snowflake across five decision criteria.

figures.title: Architecture Decision Tree
figures.purpose: Guide startup architecture challenge with branching criteria.
figures.layout: Top-down decision tree: workload type → consistency needs → scale → recommendation
figures.caption: Figure 1.11: Decision tree for selecting operational, analytical, and AI data platforms.
figures.alt: Decision tree starting with transaction volume question and branching to database recommendations.
figures.animationNotes: Reveal branches on click; leaf nodes show technology icons.

figures.title: Textbook Chapter Roadmap
figures.purpose: Preview Modules 1–6 so students see where Chapter 1 fits in the learning journey.
figures.layout: Horizontal roadmap with Module 1 highlighted; chapter titles as milestones
figures.caption: Figure 1.12: Chapter 1 opens a journey from foundations through SQL, NoSQL, architecture, and AI.
figures.alt: Roadmap showing 34 chapters across six modules with Chapter 1 highlighted in Module 1.
figures.animationNotes: Pan right to preview future modules; return highlight to CH-001.
